title: Dryopteris drakei
intro: Dryopteris drakei, also known as Drake's oak fern, is a species of fern in the family Dryopteridaceae, characterized by its large, lance-shaped fronds. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin irritations, due to its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Dryopteris drakei include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, the plant is often prepared as a tea, tincture, or powder, with the most common preparations being a decoction of the leaves and a salve made from the plant's extracts, used to treat skin conditions and wounds.
taxonomy: Dryopteris drakei is classified within the family Dryopteridaceae. This species belongs to the genus Dryopteris and is a type of fern. It is further classified as a member of the order Polypodiales and class Polypodiopsida. Dryopteris drakei is also a part of the division Pteridophyta. The classification of Dryopteris drakei reflects its evolutionary relationships and position within the plant kingdom.
uses: Dryopteris drakei is a species of fern that has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant is believed to have anti-inflammatory properties, which may help in reducing swelling and pain. In some cultures, it is used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and constipation, due to its potential ability to regulate bowel movements. Additionally, the plant may have antimicrobial properties, making it useful in treating infections. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Dryopteris drakei for medicinal purposes.

preparations: Dryopteris drakei is a species of fern, and information about its herbal preparations is limited.
